coredata.dc:title: Extensive solar light harvesting by integrating UPCL C-dots with Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2: Highly efficient photocatalytic degradation toward amoxicillin
coredata.dc:creator: Le, Shukun
Yang, Weishan
Chen, Gonglai
Yan, Aoyu
Wang, Xiaojing
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (C-dots) mediated Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 heterostructures with spongy structure were successfully assembled by simple hydrothermal route. The photocatalytic removal efficiency of amoxicillin (AMX, 20 mg L−1) over C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 was estimated to reach up 88.3% within 120 min simulated solar light irradiating. Meanwhile, the HPLC-MS/MS analysis and density functional theory (DFT) computation were examined to clarify the photo-degradation pathway of AMX. The mechanism investigation proposed that with the modification of C-dots, the photocatalysts improves the utilization of solar energy by harvesting the long wavelength solar light due to their unique up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL). In addition, the porous spongy structure and plenty of tiny C-dots promote the ability of adsorption by enlarged specific surface area. Furthermore, the C-dots mediated Z-type heterojunction of Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 facilitates the efficient separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers. Our work affords a promising approach for the design of the high-efficient photocatalysts to remedy poisonous antibiotics in aqueous environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2
Photocatalytic degradation
Removal of amoxicillin
Up-converted photoluminescence
Wide solar light harvesting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Details of experimental process, characterization and theoretical calculation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation mechanism of the C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 nanocomposite.
XRD patterns of the as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples.
HRTEM of C-dots (a), SEM (b), TEM (c) and HRTEM (d) of 7CSS-1.5.
XPS spectra of (a) O 1s of SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples; (b) C 1s of the SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples.

Photo-degradation rates of (a) AMX with P25, SnO2, STO, SS-1.5, 7CSS-1.5 and blank under simulated sun light and in the absence of light, (b) STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 at different pH values; (c) TOC with 7CSS-1.5; (d) Recycling experiments of photocatalytic AMX degradation over SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 under simulate solar light irradiation.

The atoms with larger Fukui index marked with pink color (a) and photo-degradation pathway (b) of AMX. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The adsorption capacity of SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5; (b) Effect of pH on adsorption of AMX on 7CSS-1.5; (c) Chemical structure of AMX under different pH values; (d) Zeta potential of STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5.

(a) Photo-degradation rates under different light illumination; (b) UV–vis absorption and the insert is the band gap energy for SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5; (c) Transient photocurrent response for STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 under different light illumination; (d) Mott−Schottky plots of SnO2 and STO.
The possible mechanism of the C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 nanocomposite removal of AMX under simulate solar light illumination.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Baoshan Xing.

head.ce:title: Extensive solar light harvesting by integrating UPCL C-dots with Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2: Highly efficient photocatalytic degradation toward amoxicillin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Physics of Rare Earth Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Huhhot, 010021, Inner Mongolia, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Physics of Rare Earth Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Physics of Rare Earth Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Huhhot, 010021, Inner Mongolia, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (C-dots) mediated Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 heterostructures with spongy structure were successfully assembled by simple hydrothermal route. The photocatalytic removal efficiency of amoxicillin (AMX, 20 mg L−1) over C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 was estimated to reach up 88.3% within 120 min simulated solar light irradiating. Meanwhile, the HPLC-MS/MS analysis and density functional theory (DFT) computation were examined to clarify the photo-degradation pathway of AMX. The mechanism investigation proposed that with the modification of C-dots, the photocatalysts improves the utilization of solar energy by harvesting the long wavelength solar light due to their unique up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL). In addition, the porous spongy structure and plenty of tiny C-dots promote the ability of adsorption by enlarged specific surface area. Furthermore, the C-dots mediated Z-type heterojunction of Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 facilitates the efficient separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers. Our work affords a promising approach for the design of the high-efficient photocatalysts to remedy poisonous antibiotics in aqueous environment.

ce:section.ce:para: The water environment was increasingly damaged over past years by antibiotics main from pharmaceutical industries and hospitals, which have serious threats towards human health and ecosystem (Wang et al., 2019a,b,c; Adhikari et al., 2019). Because of the special stability of these antibiotics pollutants with lots of complicated aromatics in their molecules, it is exceptionally difficult to smash them by traditional treatment methods (Zhang et al., 2019). Recently, the photocatalytic technology over semiconductors has become a most promising and environmental friendly ways to remove antibiotics by utilizing solar energy (Ju et al., 2017; Kusiak-Nejman and Morawski, 2019; Jiang et al., 2019). Among these researches, many photocatalysts were generally focused on UV lights and visible lights. However, the near-infrared (NIR) light (780 nm < λ < 2500 nm) contained by almost 52% in the solar spectra was barely utilized (Wang et al., 2019a,b,c). Obviously, it is significantly rewarding to design the up-conversion luminescence material phototcatalysts which could effectively expand solar light harvest by adsorbing NIR light and incidentally emitting an UV or visible light. However, the possible issue is the strong luminescence property may cause quickly recombination of the photo-generated carriers, which will further limit the quantum efficiency in the practical application of available photocatalysts (Zhu et al., 2019). As we know, constructing heterojunction by compositing two suitable semiconductors is an efficient method to inhibit the photo-generated carriers’ recombination and promote transference (Xue et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). In recent literature, majority of the semiconductor nanocomposites have contributed to the quickly separation of the photo-excited electrons and holes and the improved reactivity, such as TiO2/CdS (Low et al., 2019), WO3/Ag3PO4 (Lu et al., 2017), C3N4/ZnO (Le et al., 2017), BiOCl/TiO2 (Ouyang et al., 2018) and Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 (Lang et al., 2015). Nevertheless, such composites may fail to secure highly redox ability because holes (or electrons) tend to cut down part of their oxidative (or reductive) potentials during the transfer process. Thus, it seems to be very hard task to degrade AMX due to its stable structure which needs a sufficient oxidative potential. Generally, the Z-scheme heterostructure established by two semiconductors and an electron mediator have been proved to maintain simultaneously the effective carriers’ separation and highly redox potential.

Sn2Ta2O7 is a class of characteristic wide-gap semiconductor (4.0 eV) with the advantages of high chemical stability and extremely positive electrochemical potential (Hosogi et al., 2008). It has reported Sn2Ta2O7 exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance with UV light irradiation (Hase et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a,b,c). Interestingly, it has been found in our previous work (Lang et al., 2015) when compositing with SnO2 (also a good wide-gap photocatalyst), the variable valence states of tin ions all exist in two independent components. That is, a coupled Sn2+-Sn4+ redox appeared by the valence reduction of Sn4+ to Sn2+ in SnO2 meanwhile a valence oxidation from Sn2+ to Sn4+ in Sn2Ta2O7. Thus, the efficient electron-hole separation and excellent visible-light absorption through an in-situ redox between two components have been achieved. Therefore, Sn2Ta2O7 and SnO2 should be the most preferred participants in our Z-type system to be assembled. As for the electron mediator, the ionic redox shuttle, such as IO3 −/I− is inconvenient for the actual waste water remedy (Suzuki et al., 2017). In addition, the solid electron mediator, such as Pt, Au and Ag are often chosen to construct full solid Z-scheme photocatalytic systems due to their excellent electron transmission and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) (Yin et al., 2017). However, it is limited due to the scarcity and valuableness, also instability due to their agglomeration and shedding. C-dots is a novel zero-dimensional carbon-related material due to its non-toxic, low cost, unique up-converted photoluminescence (PL), excellent photo-generated electron transfer and prominent photo-induced electron reserve characters (Liu et al., 2019a,b; Hou et al., 2019). To date, various types of C-dots based semiconductor photocatalysts had generally exploited for photo-degradation such as TiO2 (Li et al., 2018), MoS2 (Jiao et al., 2019), WO3 (Song et al., 2017) and CQDs/m-BiVO4 (Liu et al., 2019a,b). Notably, tiny C-dots are well approbatory to be utilized as solid electron-bridged mediation in Z-scheme photocatalytic systems in considering its several great traits such as the superfine size and electron reserve inherency which contribute to the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency with good reusable stability. In addition, the introduction of C-dots is believed to come true in extending the absorbing solar light to NIR wavelength. All things considered, C-dots combined with Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 could be regarded as a wonderful system to achieve effective photocatalytic remedy of antibiotics wastewater under the simulated sun light.

In this investigation, we have synthesized C-dots modified Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 heterojunction photocatalysts through a one-step hydrothermal method. Amoxicillin (AMX), one of the most popular β-lactam antibiotics, was used as model antibiotic to evaluate the removal efficiency of C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 nanocomposite under simulated solar light. The density functional theory (DFT) and high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometer (HPLC-MS/MS) were utilized to investigate the intermediates and photo-degradation pathway of AMX. Moreover, the removal of AMX under various pH conditions and different light resources were tested to speculate the synergy mechanism. This work provided an insight for the design of C-dots mediated Z-type photocatalytic system with widen solar light harvest, quickly carriers’ separation, and highly remained redox ability in the application of the antibiotics removal.

The chemicals used in this study, stannous chloride (SnCl2·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), hydrofluoric acid (HF), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), ethanol (C2H6O) were purchased from Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd in Tianjin, China. Tantalum pentoxide (Ta2O5) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd in Shanghai, China. AMX was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. The characteristic and CAS number of all chemical reagents and AMX was listed in Table S1. All chemical reagents in this work were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in this work was deionized and from laboratory preparation. C-dots were synthesized by “bottom-up” approaches according to our previous publication (Le et al., 2019). The detail fabrication of C-dots solution was provided in supporting information (Text 1. Additional Details on Methods).

The fabrication strategy for C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 was illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a typical procedure, Ta2O5 (0.5 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL HF (5 wt %) to obtained H2TaF7. Adding NH3 (28 wt%) to adjust pH = 7, in order to hydrolyze of H2TaF7. Subsequently, SnCl2·2H2O (1.5 mmol) was added into the suspension. NaOH solution (4 mol L−1) was wisely dropped into the mixture solution under magnetic stirring until the yellow precipitate appeared. Accordingly, cooling down naturally, the yellow product was repeatedly washed with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at 353 K for 18 h. Based on the above methods, the different molar ratios of Sn and Ta at 1.2:1, 1.5:1 and 2.0:1 were denoted as SS-1.2, SS-1.5 and SS-2.0, respectively. Next, the prepared C-dots (1.35 mg mL−1, 2, 10, 14 and 19 mL) were added into the mixture to control the weight of the C-dots being 1, 5, 7 and 9 wt% in the composites, respectively. The resultant mixture was continuously stirred for 8 h and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon liner of stainless steel autoclave to heated at 433 K for 24 h in an oven. A series of C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 nanocomposites with different contents of C-dots were prepared, labeled as nCSS-1.5 for simplicity, where n represented the mass ratio of C-dots additive amount. For a comparative study, Sn2Ta2O7 and SnO2 were prepared according as the reported references (Lang et al., 2015) denoted as STO and SnO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Details of experimental process, characterization and theoretical calculation
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental process of adsorption and photo-degradation, DFT calculation and the characterization were provided in supporting information (Text 1. Additional Details on Methods).

XRD analyses were applied to determine the crystalline phases of as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples, and the resulting XRD spectra were shown in Fig. 2 . Besides, the XRD spectra of the as-prepared binary and ternary samples with different compositing rates were seen in Fig. S1. The diffraction peaks at 14.63°, 28.22°, 29.50°, 34.20°, 44.91°, 49.14°, 57.36°, 61.23°, 63.32° and 66.74° are indexed to cubic Sn2Ta2O7 (JCPDS No. 74-1353) (Velikokhatnyi et al., 2009). Other peaks at 26.59°, 33.89°, 37.96°, 38.99°, 42.65°, 51.79°, 54.77° and 56.86° are identical to tetragonal SnO2 (JCPDS No. 72-1147) (Lu et al., 2019). From XRD analysis of Fig. 2 and Fig. S1, we can conclude that the SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 nanocomposites are predominant constituted by Sn2Ta2O7 and SnO2. Moreover, the characteristic peak corresponding to carbon at 26° is not clearly observed because of its low content, high dispersion and low crystalline intensity.

To research the morphology of as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples, both SEM and TEM were employed and the micrographs were reported in Fig. 3 and Fig. S2. From Fig. 3a and the inset image there, it is obvious that the as-prepared C-dots are homogeneous spheroidal and well dispersed with a diameter average around 9.5 nm. The SEM and TEM reported in Fig. S2 clearly reveals the SS-1.5 is spongy morphology assembled with the amorphous SnO2 and Sn2Ta2O7. For 7CSS-1.5, tiny C-dots are evenly scattered over the surface of SnO2 and Sn2Ta2O7 (Fig. 3b and c). Furthermore, as shown in HRTEM (Fig. 3d), three types of lattice spacing about 0.322 nm, 0.301 nm and 0.336 nm are corresponding to the (0 0 2) facet of C-dots (Zhang et al., 2019), (2 2 2) facet of Sn2Ta2O7 (Suzuki et al., 2019) and (1 1 0) facet of SnO2 (Fu et al., 2019), which is a strong evidence for the formation of C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 heterojunctions (C-dots size around 9.5 nm). These results are in accordance with XRD (Fig. 2).

To further insight into the chemical compositions of the as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples, the XPS analysis was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S3. It is proved that Ta5+ and Ta4+ exist for all samples, either sole Sn2Ta2O7 or binary and ternary composites, inferring there are lots of oxygen vacancy (Fig. S3 and the text analysis there). Furthermore, both Sn4+ and Sn2+ ions are proved to coexist in the as-prepared SS and CSS samples due to the self-doping through in situ redox of Sn2+-Sn4+ pairs as reported in our previous work (Lang et al., 2015). As we all know, the self-doping will build an impurity band which could harvest the visible light response (Lang et al., 2015). Fig. 4a displays the O 1s XPS spectra for as-prepared samples. Obviously, Ta–O, Sn–O, and hydroxyl groups could be identified at 529.7 eV, 530.6 eV and 531.5 eV in both SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5. In addition, a new peak at 532.6 eV appears which corresponds to CO groups formed by C-dots and oxygen species on the surface, consistent with the reference (Shi et al., 2019). Fig. 4b shows the C 1s XPS spectra for SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5. The two peaks at 286.0 eV and 284.7 eV are ascribed to C–OH/C–O–C groups and C–C, consistent with the references (Ma et al., 2019). Importantly, a typical peak observed at 288.4 eV for 7CSS-1.5 is corresponded to CO groups due to the participation of C-dots. Unquestionably, it is evidenced C-dots have been well united with the O species on the surface of the photocatalysts which will contribute to the sustained reactivity by effectively inhibiting C-dots’ agglomeration and shedding. Meanwhile, the relative atomic percentage (Atomic %) of as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples were also tested by XPS and the results were shown in Table S2. It can be seen that all samples exhibit reasonable stoichiometry ratio to prove the formation of C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 heterojunction with the initial added dosages, consistently with XRD and TEM.

The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples was evaluated with AMX solution (20 mg L−1) under simulate solar light and the results was shown in Fig. 5 a together with the degradation of AMX without any catalysts or in the absence of light for comparison purpose. Clearly, AMX is stable with solar light and it is barely degraded in the case without any photocatalysts. Then, the photo-degrade AMX were carried out for 150 min with the addition of the as-prepared samples under simulate solar light after the adsorption for 60 min in dark to ensure the adsorption equilibrium. It is observed that the best photocatalytic activity of 88.3% is achieved over the composite with the optimal molar ratios 1.5:1 of Sn and Ta and 7 wt% addition contents of C-dots, which is 5.2, 4.5, 3.9 and 1.4 times higher than that of P25, SnO2, STO and SS-1.5, respectively (Fig.5a, Fig. S5). Furthermore, the photocatalytic kinetics were obtained to further research the photo-degradation of AMX. From Fig. S6a, all samples is proved to be a linear relationship between irradiation time and ln(C0/Ct) and the 7CSS-1.5 presents the highest rate constant k (0.0204 min−1).

The photodegradation rate of 7CSS-1.5 toward different initial concentrations of AMX, dosage of photocatalyst, and reactive temperature were also tested. The degradation rate is 93.9%, 91.6%, 88.3%, and 65.5%, under 150 min irradiation for 5, 10, 20, and 30 mg L−1 of AMX, respectively as shown in Fig. S4b. Obviously, the photodegradation performance decreases with an initial concentration of AMX from 5 up to 30 mg L−1. Additionally, the effect of catalyst dosage on the degraded efficiency is showed in Fig. S4c. When the dosage of the photocatalyst increases from 0.3 to 0.5 g L−1, the photocatalytic activity increases whereas it is decreased if the dosage is raising up to 0.6 g L−1. The lost activity with the higher amount of the catalyst is explained as the more dosage could increase the turbidity of solution which may inhibit the penetration of light and also could cause agglomeration and sedimentation of the photocatalyst, leading to lower active sites. Hence, the optimized dosage of 7CSS-1.5 was set up as 0.5 g L−1. The reactive temperature is also importantly affected the photodegraded efficiency. The tests were conducted by varying the temperature within the range of 15–45 °C (Fig. S4d). It is observed the photocatalytic activity increases from 0 to 35 °C, and then a decrease at 45 °C. Thus, for the optimized efficiency to cost, the room temperature (25 °C) was chosen in the present investigation.

Because of the existential form of AMX is different under acidic or alkaline condition, the photocatalytic degradation of 7CSS-1.5 was researched at different pH values (2, 7 and 10). As shown in Fig. 5b and 89.6% of AMX is degraded after 150 min simulate solar light illumination under the acidic condition while it is 85.5% in alkaline condition and 88.3% in the neutral environment. Therefore, 7CSS-1.5 presents the outstanding photocatalytic performance for AMX removal at acidic, alkaline, or natural condition, implying that the photocatalysts afford a hopeful application for wastewater treatment under the practical environments with various contaminant.

In addition, to further identify the mineralization ability of AMX solution, the total organic carbon (TOC) analyses were investigated with 7CSS-1.5 under simulated solar light. As shown in Fig. 5c, the TOC of AMX solution with 7CSS-1.5 sample is reduced from 18.95 to 6.95 mg L−1 whereas the total inorganic carbon (TIC) increase from 0 to 4.37 mg L−1 during 210 min reactive process. Actually, the TOC removal efficiency (63.3%) is lower than the AMX photo-degradation rate (88.3%) on the same condition, suggesting that it is difficult to completely destroy the organic group of AMX into CO2 and H2O. Moreover, both TOC and total carbon (TC) are decreased at the first 60 min because of the particular pre-adsorption effect by 7CSS-1.5 sample. Generally, the reduction of TC and TOC accompanied with the increase of TIC smoothly indicates that 7CSS-1.5 sample could act as a promising full spectrum photocatalytic material.

Besides, the photocatalytic stability of SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 nanocomposites was examined with the four recycled experiments of AMX photo-degradation under same experimental conditions (Fig. 5d). It is clearly that the degradation rate of AMX with SS-1.5 is apparent declined after four recycling experiments, however, the favorable photocatalytic degradation performance is still remained for 7CSS-1.5. Also, the XRD pattern of the original 7CSS-1.5 and the one used after four recycled experiments are shown in Fig. S6b. The main XRD peaks of 7CSS-1.5 display at unchanged positions with a little reduced intensity during the four recycles, confirming the good stability of C-dots modified SnO2 and Sn2Ta2O7 composite. Summarily, combining with the degraded efficiency, catalyst dosage, stability and practical availability, the photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared 7CSS-1.5 sample is superior to the reported photo-catalysts which have been revealed in Table S3.

To further investigate the photo-degradation intermediates of AMX with 7CSS-1.5, the high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass (HPLC-MS) analysis were examined and the results are shown in Fig. S7 and Table S4. Meanwhile, in order to clarify the photo-degradation pathway of AMX, the Fukui index described radical attacking ability (f 0) of the atoms was also calculated by DFT for AMX molecule (Fig. 6 and Table S5). As shown in Table S4, the f 0 values of S (25), O (22), O (7), C (19), C (2) and C (3) are all larger than others and marked with pink in Fig. 6a, and therefore, free radical attacks are more likely to occur at these sites. By combining the detected intermediates and Fukui index, the photo-degradation pathway of AMX is presented in Fig. 6b. As shown in the pathway, S (25) with a higher f 0 is very possible attacked by •O2 −, an active specie in the present reaction, to produce an intermediate with m/z of 318 and subsequently the intermediate (m/z of 324) via losing –NH2. Next, the intermediate of m/z = 165 is produced via attacking N (16) site with the active free radicals and intermediate (m/z = 216) via attacking N (16), where all have the larger f 0 index. Also, all above initial AMX molecules and mentioned intermediates might be further attacked and broken into other small molecules or mineralized into H2O and CO2, by referring to the lower intensity in HPLC-MS peaks. Consistently, most of the photo-degradation intermediates of AMX have also been observed in the recited references which are considered to be less toxic and harmless (Wei et al., 2020; Yadav et al., 2019; Mirzaei et al., 2019).

The pre-adsorption capacity is important for removal of AMX by enriching pollutants to accelerate photocatalytic degradation. Then, adsorption of AMX solution (20 mg L−1) over the as-prepared SnO2, STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples was evaluated (Fig. 7 a). The equilibrium adsorption is achieved after 30 min standing in dark and no further adsorption is observed with the increasing of time. The higher adsorption capacity is reached for 7CSS-1.5 (14.72 mg g−1), which is 4, 3 and 1.5 times higher compared with SnO2, STO and SS-1.5, respectively. Actually, by testing BET specific surface area (Table S6), it confirms the spongy-structure 7CSS-1.5 has larger specific surface area and thus presents excellent pre-adsorption ability. Furthermore, it is observed by measuring the adsorption isotherm of AMX, with the equilibrium concentration of AMX increasing from 2 to 12 mg L−1, the adsorption capacity (Qe) is almost linearly increased (Fig. S8). Then the saturation adsorption Qe is remained with around 14.8 mg g−1 in case the initial concentration larger than 12 mg L−1. Meanwhile, the adsorption isotherm was well fitted with Langmuir equation (Hu et al., 2019), indicating that the monolayer adsorption mode is exactly representation for the present adsorption behavior.

Additionally, the influence of AMX adsorption performance under the different initial pH value (2, 7 and 11) was also tested (Fig. 7b). It can be seen that the adsorption capacity of 7CSS-1.5 is always higher than that of STO and SS-1.5 with an optimized value at neutral solution environment and it is a little decrease at a smaller pH value (pH = 2) or a larger one (pH = 11). As we all know, the amphoteric AMX molecule has four different species at different pH ranges (Oberoi et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 7c, AMX is converted as H3AMX+ at pH < 2.4, H2AMX at 2.4 < pH < 7.4, HAMX− at 7.4 < pH < 9.4 and AMX2− at pH > 9.4. Furthermore, we found the zeta potentials of STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 (Fig. 7d) present positive value in the acid condition whereas it is negative under neutral or basic environment. Thus it is concluded that electrostatic repulsion between catalyst and AMX is increased in the acid or alkaline environment. Correlating with the best adsorption capacity under a neutral pH range, it is proposed the electrostatic interaction is the preferential adsorption mode between AMX species and the prepared samples.

Firstly, the light utilization of as-prepared samples were evaluated by AMX degradation under simulate solar light, UV light, visible light and NIR light. The photo-degradation rates of AMX are shown in Fig. 8 a. It is observed that the degradation of AMX for 7CSS-1.5 is 1.1, 1.4, 1.7 and 4.2 times higher than that of SS-1.5 under simulate solar light, UV light, visible light and NIR light irradiation respectively. Importantly as we expected, 7CSS-1.5 sample exhibits 52.9% degradation of AMX with sole NIR light illuminating while it is only 12.6% for SS-1.5. Obviously, with the C-dots introduction, 7CSS-1.5 sample significantly expands its degraded ability for AMX, which is assigned to the excess NIR light harvesting due to the up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) property of C-dots (Zhang et al., 2019).

It is also evidenced by the measured UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) (Fig. 8b). The light absorption of SnO2 and STO is located in UV light, and their composite SS-1.5 branch out to the visible light region. Furthermore, an even wide range of light absorption covering UV, visible, and NIR region is gained for 7CSS-1.5, a sample modified with C-dots. It indicates that NIR light could be harvested through UCPL of C-dots and thus the outstanding solar light utilizing for 7CSS-1.5 is achieved.

PL spectrum and the transient photocurrent response were measured to characterize the recombination and transfer behaviors of photo-generated carriers for the prepared samples. Compared with SnO2, STO and SS-1.5, the photoluminescence intensity of 7CSS-1.5 is much weaker (Fig. S9). As reported (Rehman et al., 2019), the lower intensity confirms the higher separation efficiency of photo-generated charges in 7CSS-1.5. In addition, the photocurrent of STO, SS-1.5 and 7CSS-1.5 samples was also tested with the various light resources of UV, visible and NIR light, respectively (Fig. 8c). Remarkably, 7CSS-1.5 sample displays the highest photocurrent with a few on-off cycles under all used light resources, which further confirms the photo-excited electron-hole pairs could be generated by harvesting wider solar light spectrum. Moreover, 7CSS-1.5, a sample modified with C-dots, exhibits significant highly photocurrent density response under NIR light whereas those samples without C-dots (STO and SS-1.5) remain barely photocurrent density response in the range of NIR light. It is obviously that the NIR light has been utilized to produce photocurrent via UCPL of C-dots for 7CSS-1.5. Furthermore, the photocurrent of 7CSS-1.5 under UV, visible and NIR light was reversible and stable, implying that the current can productively increase violently under each irradiation and recover rapidly in the dark.

The band potentials of the prepared samples were examined with Mott−Schottky plot for the purpose to determine the flow way of the photo-excited electron, and the results are shown in Fig. 8d. STO belongs to a n-type semiconductor with a positive slope values and its band potential is −0.25 eV while SnO2 is also a n-type one with a band potential of −0.08 eV (Fig. 8d). As we know, the Fermi level is very close to the energy level of conduct band (CB) in a n-type semiconductor. Thus the band potentials measured for STO and SnO2 are approximately considered to be consistent with their CB levels. In addition, combining with the band gap of SnO2 and STO (Insert of Fig. 8b), the valence and conductor band have been estimated and aligned as shown in Fig. S10. As above discussion, the photo-generated electrons could be produced under an expanded solar light irradiation, that is, Sn2Ta2O7 with UV, SnO2 with visible light, and C-dots with near infrared due to a UCPL. Remarkably, the photo-generated electrons will transfer from CB of Sn2Ta2O7 to the CB of SnO2 meanwhile holes still stay in Sn2Ta2O7 according to the aligned energy band of the assembly. It is known that C-dots are sub-metallic with a perfect merit for electron’s transport, thus the photo-generated electrons will subsequently transfer to C-dots and further to AMX, well inhibiting their recombination with the holes in such heterojunction. Consistently, the enhanced photocurrent response could give a support for such an electron transferred tunnel.

The reactive species scavenging experiment and electron spin resonance (ESR) technique were utilized to proof the major active species in the photocatalytic degradation of AMX. As shown in Fig. S11, when t-BuOH (butyl alcohol, 1 mmol L−1) and BQ (p-benzoquinone, 1 mmol L−1) were introduced into the reactive solution of AMX to trap •OH and •O2 −, the photocatalytic activity is significantly decreased (Raziq et al., 2018), while with the addition of EDTA (1 mmol L−1) and AgNO3 (1 mmol L−1), the photo-degradation efficiency was slightly decreased. Thus it is proposed that •OH and •O2 − radicals are the main active species while h+ and e− play an incidental role in the photo-degradation process of AMX (Liang et al., 2018). Further evidences were provided with the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique and the results were shown in Fig. S12. It is observed that none of signals appears in the dark, while the distinct signals of •OH and •O2 − are observed under the visible light. Furthermore, the signal intensity of •OH and •O2 − is enhanced with the irradiation time increasing. Accordingly, it is concluded that •OH and •O2 − is produced under light illuminating in the 7CSS-1.5 system and these species are finally used to degrade AMX.

Summarily, associating assessment of pre-adsorptive capacity, photocatalytic routes, solar light harvest, active reactive species and photo-generated carrier transport, the removal mechanism for AMX is illustrated in Fig. 9 . At pre-adsorption stage, the excellent adsorption is attributed to the spongy morphology of C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 with larger specific surface area, and the stronger electrostatic attraction between its surfaces charges and AMX species. Meanwhile, the adsorption equilibrium is reached within 30 min, which making sure the favorable contact between the AMX molecules and succeeding photo-generated carriers. The particular UCPL effect of the C-dots and the self-doping from coupled Sn2+-Sn4+ pairs, contribute to the excellent light absorption covering UV, visible, and NIR region. By aligning the energy bands of these substances, the excited electrons are easily transported following the migration tunnel from Sn2Ta2O7 to SnO2 and then to the good electron’s reservoir of C-dots, to inhibit their recombination with the remained holes. The electrons will react with the adsorbed O2 to form •O2 − and the holes are trapped by H2O/OH− to generate •OH (Monteagudo et al., 2019), all participating in AMX oxidation.

In summary, a spongy Z-type hetero-structural C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 photocatalyst has been fabricated through a facile hydrothermal method. The removal of AMX under simulated solar light by synergy effect of adsorption and photocatalysis was investigated. The better removal rate of 88.3% towards AMX is achieved over the composite 7CSS-1.5 with a good reused stability under simulated solar light and at wider pH range of acidic, alkaline and natural condition. The degraded route is also determined through measured intermediate species with HPLC-MS and Fukui index of the active atoms under the attack of reactive radicals from DFT calculation. The mechanism investigation confirms that 7CSS-1.5 presents excellent light absorption covering UV, visible, and NIR region, assigned to the self-doping from Sn2+-Sn4+ pairs and the up-converted photoluminescence introduced by C-dots. Meanwhile, the separation and transfer of the photo-generated electrons and holes are effective promoted by C-dots mediated Z-type heterojunction. In addition, C-dots/Sn2Ta2O7/SnO2 presents excellent pre-adsorptive capacity toward AMX, which could originate from the larger specific surface area, lots of adsorption sites, and strong electrostatic interaction between AMX and photocatalyst surface. Therefore, the extensive solar light utilization, strong pre-adsorption, and quickly carrier transfers all contribute to the enhanced AMX removal efficiency in present C-dots mediated Z-type heterojunction. This work offered a novel insight for the design of efficient photocatalysts to treat AMX by synergy effect of adsorption and photocatalysis under the wider solar light harvesting.

Shukun Le: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Weishan Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Gonglai Chen: Formal analysis, Visualization. Aoyu Yan: Validation, Supervision. Xiaojing Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: ☒ The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 21777078and 21567017), the Project of Research and Development of the Applied Technology for the Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region of China (201702112), the major basic research and open project of the Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region of China (30500-515330303).
